candidates.rationale: Opens with the cost crisis in multi-agent deployments: token costs scale super-linearly with topology complexity, yet practitioners pick topologies blindly with no cost awareness. Builds urgency before revealing CostRouter as the solution. Strong for ICML reviewers who value clear problem motivation and practical significance.

sections.title: Introduction
subsections: 1.1 The Cost Problem in Multi-Agent LLM Deployments
sections.argumentation_note: Open with concrete dollar-cost example: running a 3-agent debate topology on GPT-4 costs 6-12x a single call, yet delivers <10% accuracy gain on easy queries. Frame the gap: model-routing research optimizes which LLM to call, but ignores the structural cost multiplier of the topology itself. State 3 contributions.

sections.title: Related Work
sections.argumentation_note: Organize into 4 thematic clusters. For each cluster, identify what is missing: model-routing ignores topology, topology papers ignore cost, budget papers use static allocation, difficulty estimators don't route across topologies.

sections.title: CostRouter: Cost-Aware Topology Routing
sections.argumentation_note: Formalize cost-quality tradeoff as constrained optimization. Define topology cost model. Present difficulty estimator. Describe routing policy with algorithm pseudocode.

sections.title: Experimental Setup
sections.argumentation_note: State 3 research questions. Describe 4 benchmarks, 3 topologies, 6 baselines.

sections.title: Results
sections.argumentation_note: Lead with main results table showing 2.7% token cost reduction. Pareto frontier. Ablation. Difficulty calibration.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.argumentation_note: Interpret topology dominance by difficulty level. Honest failure modes. Limitations. Broader impact.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.argumentation_note: Recap contributions. Highlight 2.7% token cost savings. Forward-looking.

candidates.rationale: Opens with the key insight that topology cost is a structural property independent of the LLM backbone, meaning cost multipliers are predictable before execution. This reframes the problem from 'which model?' to 'which structure?' and makes CostRouter feel like the inevitable next step. Strongest novelty positioning for ICML's originality criterion, and the topology cost algebra gives the paper theoretical depth beyond pure empirics.

sections.title: Introduction
subsections: 1.2 From Model Routing to Topology Routing
sections.argumentation_note: Open with the insight: a debate topology with 3 agents and 4 rounds costs 24x a single call regardless of the backbone LLM, yet the accuracy gain varies from 0% on easy queries to 15% on hard queries. This structural cost is predictable, decomposable, and exploitable. Existing routing research optimizes per-call cost (which model?) but ignores the multiplicative cost of the call pattern (which topology?). CostRouter bridges this gap. State 3 contributions: (1) topology cost algebra, (2) CostRouter per-query router, (3) 2.7% token cost reduction.

sections.title: Related Work
sections.argumentation_note: Frame related work through the lens of the insight: all prior cost optimization happens at model level or within fixed topology. Position CostRouter as the first to combine topology cost modeling with per-query difficulty-aware routing and quality guarantees.

sections.title: CostRouter: Topology-Level Cost Routing
sections.argumentation_note: Start from topology cost algebra: formalize token cost decomposition per topology class. Build cost prediction model. Introduce difficulty-aware routing with lightweight classifier. Quality threshold mechanism along Pareto frontier. Theoretical bound on cost savings.

sections.title: Experimental Setup
sections.argumentation_note: Frame experiments around validating the insight. 4 RQs, 4 benchmarks, 3 topology families, 6 baselines. GPT-4o backbone, 3 seeds.

sections.title: Results and Analysis
sections.argumentation_note: First validate cost algebra. Then main comparison table: 2.7% token cost reduction. Pareto frontier. Ablation. Scaling analysis.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.argumentation_note: Return to opening insight and confirm experiments validated it. Where cost model breaks down. Honest limitations. Broader implications for cost democratization.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.argumentation_note: Restate insight. Summarize 3 contributions and 2.7% token cost reduction. Look forward to learned topology synthesis and online adaptation.

candidates.rationale: Opens with a surprising empirical finding: on easy queries, a single LLM call matches a 3-agent debate at 1/24th the cost, while on hard queries the debate is worth every token. Builds the paper around explaining and exploiting this observation. High engagement hook but risks feeling purely empirical without theoretical grounding.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.argumentation_note: Open with motivating scatter plot: accuracy vs cost for 3 topologies across 500 queries. Easy queries cluster at high-accuracy/low-cost for flat topology.

sections.title: Related Work
sections.argumentation_note: Organize around the evidence gap: many papers report costs or optimize topologies, but none connect per-query difficulty to topology cost-effectiveness.

sections.title: From Evidence to Router: CostRouter
sections.argumentation_note: Start from empirical observation. Derive cost model. Build difficulty estimator. Present routing algorithm. Quality guarantees.

sections.title: Experimental Evaluation
sections.argumentation_note: Experiments designed to generalize the opening observation.

sections.title: Results
sections.argumentation_note: Show difficulty-cost pattern holds on all benchmarks. Main cost reduction. Pareto. Sensitivity. Ablation.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.argumentation_note: Synthesize evidence. Routing helps most with heterogeneous difficulty distributions. Limitations. Practical guidance.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.argumentation_note: Return to opening evidence. Recap CostRouter and 40-60% savings. Future directions.

selection_rationale: Selected candidate_b (insight-first) with weighted score 8.35. Beats candidate_a on novelty (9 vs 7) and engagement (8 vs 7). Beats candidate_c on flow (8 vs 7) and novelty (9 vs 7). The insight-first strategy positions the topology cost algebra as a theoretical contribution that elevates the paper beyond a pure systems/empirical study. For ICML, where originality scores 1-4 are critical for acceptance, leading with the structural insight that topology cost is predictable and decomposable before execution gives reviewers a clear novelty hook. The cost algebra also provides the theoretical grounding (Section 3.5 Theoretical Cost Savings Bound) that ICML reviewers expect, distinguishing this from a purely empirical approach.

sections.title: Introduction
subsections: 1.2 From Model Routing to Topology Routing
sections.argumentation_note: Open with the insight: a debate topology with 3 agents and 4 rounds costs 24x a single call regardless of the backbone LLM, yet the accuracy gain varies from 0% on easy queries to 15% on hard queries. This structural cost is predictable, decomposable, and exploitable. Existing routing research (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM) optimizes per-call cost (which model?) but ignores the multiplicative cost of the call pattern (which topology?). CostRouter bridges this gap by lifting routing from the model level to the topology level. State 3 contributions: (1) a topology cost algebra decomposing multi-agent token spend, (2) CostRouter: per-query topology selection minimizing cost subject to quality threshold, (3) 2.7% token cost reduction empirically validated across 4 benchmarks.

sections.title: Related Work
sections.argumentation_note: Frame related work through the lens of the insight: all prior cost optimization happens at the model level (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, AutoMix, HybridLLM, C3PO, SCORE) or within a fixed topology (AgentDropout, Optima, AgentDiet). Topology papers (AgentBalance, BAMAS, GTD, MASS, AgentConductor, AnyMAC) optimize structure offline but lack per-query routing. Difficulty estimators (DAO, scaling laws, MasRouter) adapt workflow depth but not topology family. Position CostRouter as the first to combine topology cost modeling with per-query difficulty-aware routing and quality guarantees.

sections.title: CostRouter: Topology-Level Cost Routing
sections.argumentation_note: Start from the topology cost algebra: formalize how token cost decomposes into agents x rounds x context growth for each topology class (flat: c_base; hierarchical: c_base * N_agents * depth; debate: c_base * N_agents^2 * rounds). Show these cost formulas can be pre-computed from topology structure alone. Build the cost prediction model from this algebra. Then introduce difficulty-aware routing: a lightweight classifier maps query embeddings to difficulty scores, which index into pre-computed cost-quality curves per topology. The quality threshold mechanism selects the cheapest topology whose expected quality exceeds tau along the Pareto frontier. Provide theoretical bound: if difficulty estimator has accuracy >= alpha, CostRouter saves at least (1-alpha) * cost_gap between cheapest and most expensive topology. Include algorithm pseudocode.

sections.title: Experimental Setup
sections.argumentation_note: Frame experiments around validating the insight: (RQ1) Is topology cost truly predictable from structure alone? (RQ2) Does per-query routing outperform static topology assignment? (RQ3) How close to the oracle Pareto frontier does CostRouter get? (RQ4) How does routing performance degrade with difficulty estimation noise? Use 4 benchmarks (GAIA, MATH, HumanEval, WebArena), 3 topology families (flat, hierarchical, debate), 6 baselines (fixed topologies, FrugalGPT-adapted, BAMAS-adapted, oracle). GPT-4o backbone, 3 seeds per experiment.

sections.title: Results and Analysis
sections.argumentation_note: First validate the cost algebra: predicted vs actual token counts per topology (R^2 > 0.95). Then main comparison table: CostRouter achieves 2.7% token cost reduction at matched accuracy across all benchmarks. Pareto frontier showing CostRouter traces the efficient frontier closely. Ablation: remove difficulty estimator, remove quality threshold, remove cost model (use uniform cost assumption). Scaling analysis: does the advantage grow with more topology candidates in the pool?

sections.title: Discussion
sections.argumentation_note: Return to the opening insight and show the experiments validated it: topology cost is indeed structural and predictable, and exploiting this with per-query routing yields large cost savings. Discuss where the cost model breaks down: heterogeneous backbone mixes, adaptive topologies that change mid-execution, topologies with early stopping. Honest limitations: assumes fixed topology catalog, difficulty estimator needs labeled calibration data, GPT-4o backbone only, English-only benchmarks, no latency optimization. Broader implications: cost awareness enables multi-agent systems at organizations that cannot afford always-on debate topologies.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.argumentation_note: Restate the insight that topology cost is structural and exploitable. Summarize 3 contributions: topology cost algebra, CostRouter, and 2.7% token cost reduction. Look forward to learned topology synthesis, joint model-topology co-routing, and online cost model adaptation.

figure_plan.description: Topology cost decomposition figure: shows 3 topology families (flat, hierarchical, debate) with their token cost formulas overlaid. Bar chart comparing cost multiplier (1x, Nx, N^2x) alongside accuracy gain by difficulty level (easy/medium/hard). Visually demonstrates the structural cost insight.

CostRouter architecture diagram: query enters the difficulty estimator, which produces a difficulty score. The score indexes into pre-computed cost-quality curves per topology. The quality threshold mechanism selects the cheapest topology above tau. Output is the selected topology that executes the query.

Algorithm pseudocode for CostRouter routing: inputs (query, topology pool, quality threshold tau), steps (embed query, estimate difficulty, compute expected cost and quality per topology, filter by quality >= tau, select min cost), output (selected topology).

Table of experimental setup: benchmarks (GAIA, MATH, HumanEval, WebArena) with task counts, difficulty distributions, domain characteristics. Topology candidates with parameter configurations. Baselines with brief descriptions.

Main results table: accuracy and token cost for each method (CostRouter, 3 fixed topologies, FrugalGPT-adapted, BAMAS-adapted, oracle) across 4 benchmarks. Includes cost reduction percentage and quality delta vs best fixed topology. Confidence intervals from 3 seeds.

Pareto frontier plot: x-axis = total token cost, y-axis = accuracy. Each fixed topology is a single point. CostRouter traces a curve (by varying tau) that dominates fixed topologies. Oracle frontier shown as upper bound. Separate subplot per benchmark.

Cost model validation scatter plot: x-axis = predicted token count from topology cost algebra, y-axis = actual token count. Points colored by topology family. R^2 annotation. Shows the cost algebra accurately predicts actual costs.

Ablation bar chart: CostRouter full system vs remove-difficulty-estimator vs remove-quality-threshold vs remove-cost-model vs random-routing. Shows cost and accuracy for each ablation. Demonstrates each component contributes.
